revision.text:
[[co:commons:Category:Wakamiya-hachimangu (Hitachiota)]][[ja:若宮八幡宮 (常陸太田市)]] [[co:commons:Category:Wakamiya-hachimangu (Hitachiota)]][[ja:若宮八幡宮 (常陸太田市)]] [[sn:commons:Category:Wakamiya-hachimangu (Hitachiota)]][[ja:若宮八幡宮 (常陸太田市)]] {{Unreferenced|date=2019-03-12}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name=Wakamiya Hachimangū (若宮八幡宮) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=File:Wakamiyahachimaｎgu.JPG |location={{ill|Hitachiōta, Ibaraki|en|Hitachiōta, Ibaraki|WD=Q838663|lt=Hitachiōta|1=Hitachiōta, Ibaraki}}, {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|en|Ibaraki Prefecture|WD=Q83273|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|1=Ibaraki Prefecture}} Miyamoto-chō 2344 |coordinates={{Coord|36.543|N|140.52072222222222|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |enshrined_deities=[[Ōsasagi no Mikoto]] ({{ill|Emperor Nintoku|en|Emperor Nintoku|WD=Q313119|lt=Emperor Nintoku|1=Emperor Nintoku}})
[[Ukanomitama no Mikoto]] |established={{ill|Ōei|en|Ōei|WD=Q1193078|lt=Ōei era|1=Ōei}} (circa 1400) |shrine_Rank=Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} |annual_festival=See [[#Festivals]] |main_hall= |map={{location map|Japan Ibaraki|label=Wakamiya Hachimangū|coordinates= {{coord2|36|32|34.8|N|140|31|14.6|E|}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|36|32|34.8|N|140|31|14.6|E}}}} |prefecture={{ill|Hitachiota|WD=Q838663|1=Hitachiōta, Ibaraki}} }} [[File:Torii of Wakamiya-hachimangu (Hitachiota).jpg|thumb|right|245px|Torii]] [[File:Shinmon of Wakamiya-hachimangu (Hitachiota).jpg|thumb|right|245px|Shinmon (gate)]] '''Wakamiya Hachimangū''' (若宮八幡宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Hitachiōta, Ibaraki|en|Hitachiōta, Ibaraki|WD=Q838663|lt=Hitachiōta|1=Hitachiōta, Ibaraki}}, {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|en|Ibaraki Prefecture|WD=Q83273|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|1=Ibaraki Prefecture}}, Japan. The shrine's Rank under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|en|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|WD=Q712534|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. == History == The shrine was established during the {{ill|Ōei|en|Ōei|WD=Q1193078|lt=Ōei era|1=Ōei}} (circa 1400) by {{ill|Yoshihito Satake|ja|佐竹義人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshihito Satake|1=Satake Yoshihito|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11383421}}, who invited its deity from {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|en|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|WD=Q701403|lt=Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|1=Tsurugaoka Hachimangū}} in {{ill|Kamakura|en|Kamakura|WD=Q200267|lt=Kamakura|1=Kamakura}}, {{ill|Kanagawa Prefecture|en|Kanagawa Prefecture|WD=Q127513|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}. It was originally enshrined within {{ill|Ōta Castle (Hitachi Province)|ja|太田城 (常陸国)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōta Castle (Hitachi Province)|1=Ōda Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5369726}} as a guardian deity and served as the prayer site for the {{ill|Satake clan|en|Satake clan|WD=Q2975363|lt=Satake clan|1=Satake clan}}. Research suggests that the Satake clan initially revered a {{ill|Hachiman shrine|lt=Hachiman shrine|lt=Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八幡宮}} derived from {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|en|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|WD=Q710098|lt=Iwashimizu Hachimangū|1=Iwashimizu Hachimangū}}, which was the guardian shrine of the Minamoto clan in Hitachi. However, Yoshihito Satake, who had ties with the {{ill|Uesugi clan|en|Uesugi clan|WD=Q1150058|lt=Uesugi clan|1=Uesugi clan}}, instead adopted the enshrinement of Tsurugaoka Hachimangū, possibly against opposition from the Satake family.Teiko Tsutsumi, "Satake Clan and Hachiman Worship," {{ill|Ibaraki Prefectural History Museum Bulletin|ja|茨城県立歴史館報|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ibaraki Prefectural History Museum Bulletin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} No. 28, 2001. Compiled in {{ill|Research Series on Medieval Kanto Warriors Vol. 30: Hitachi Satake Clan|ja|シリーズ・中世関東武士の研究 第三十巻 常陸佐竹氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Research Series on Medieval Kanto Warriors Vol. 30: Hitachi Satake Clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Kōshōshō Publishing, 2021), pp. 192–227. Although the Satake clan was later relocated to {{ill|Akita|ja|秋田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita|1=Q11256953|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11256953}} after the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara|1=Battle of Sekigahara}}, they took the shrine's deity with them to {{ill|Ōdate Castle|ja|大館城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōdate Castle|1=Ōdate Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11441913}} as its guardian spirit. The deity was subsequently enshrined at {{ill|Ōdate Hachiman Shrine|ja|大館八幡神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōdate Hachiman Shrine|1=Ōdate Hachiman Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21606446}}, continuing its role as a protector.{{Cite web|title=八幡神社 |url=http://akita-jinjacho.sakura.ne.jp/tatsujin_etc/kennsaku/oodate/67_hachiman_hachiman.html |publisher=Akita Prefecture Shrine Association |accessdate=2015-10-12}} In {{ill|Keichō|en|Keichō|WD=Q1328185|lt=Keichō|1=Keichō}} 14 (1609), the first {{ill|Daimyo|en|Daimyo|WD=Q61982|lt=daimyō|1=Daimyo}} of the {{ill|Mito Domain|en|Mito Domain|WD=Q1146904|lt=Mito Domain|1=Mito Domain}}, {{ill|Tokugawa Yorifusa|en|Tokugawa Yorifusa|WD=Q1133680|lt=Tokugawa Yorifusa|1=Tokugawa Yorifusa}}, was healed of an illness after praying at the shrine. Thereafter, the {{ill|Mito Tokugawa family|en|Mito Tokugawa family|WD=Q842246|lt=Mito Tokugawa clan|1=Mito Tokugawa family}} held the shrine in high reverence. In {{ill|Genroku|en|Genroku|WD=Q1325001|lt=Genroku|1=Genroku}} 5 (1692), the second daimyō, {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|en|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|WD=Q1194944|lt=Tokugawa Mitsukuni|1=Tokugawa Mitsukuni}} (popularly known as Mito Kōmon), designated the shrine as a guardian of the Ōta district. In {{ill|Hōei|en|Hōei|WD=Q1145262|lt=Hōei|1=Hōei}} 5 (1708), the shrine was relocated to its current location alongside the {{ill|Ōta Inari Shrine|ja|太田稲荷神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōta Inari Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which had been worshiped together with it. In 1940 (Showa 15), based on the tradition that a woman named Tsuruko accompanied the deity’s transfer from Tsurugaoka Hachimangū and oversaw its rituals, the shrine's parishioner priestesses revived the "Tsuruko Dance." Recently, this {{ill|Kagura|en|Kagura|WD=Q1348260|lt=kagura|1=Kagura}} offering has been expanded to include performances such as {{ill|Urayasu no Mai|ja|浦安の舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urayasu no Mai|1=Urayasu no mai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11558194}}, {{ill|Toyo no Sakai no Mai|ja|豊栄の舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyo no Sakai no Mai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Yaotome no Mai|ja|八乙女舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yaotome no Mai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 巫女舞|1=miko mai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11479841}}, which are performed with grace during major festivals. [[File:Kaguraden of Wakamiya-hachimangu (Hitachiota).jpg|thumb|right|245px|Kaguraden]] == Enshrined Deities == * '''[[Ōsasagi no Mikoto]]''' (Emperor Nintoku) * '''[[Ukanomitama no Mikoto]]''' == Festivals == * January: {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|元旦祭|Saitan-sai=Saitan-sai|lt=Saitan-sai (New Year's Festival)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitan-sai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 歳旦祭}} * February: {{ill|Setsubun|ja|節分追儺祭|lt=Setsubun Tsuina-sai|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Setsubun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * May: festival * June: {{ill|Nagoshi no Harae|ja|夏越の祓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagoshi no Harae|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大祓|1=Ōharae-shiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11261910}} * September: Autumn Festival * December: {{ill|Ōharae-shiki|en|Ōharae-shiki|WD=Q11261910|lt=Ōharae|1=Ōharae-shiki}} == Cultural Properties == * Large {{ill|Zelkova serrata|en|Zelkova serrata|WD=Q920344|lt=zelkova|1=Zelkova serrata}} tree (designated a natural monument of Ibaraki Prefecture) * Hanging scroll of Hachiman as a monk * Stone sword artifacts * Military fan (attributed to {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshimitsu|en|Minamoto no Yoshimitsu|WD=Q3083118|lt=Minamoto no Yoshimitsu|1=Minamoto no Yoshimitsu}}) == Auxiliary Shrines == * {{ill|Kitano Tenjin Shrine|ja|北野天神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitano Tenjin Shrine|1=Kitano Tenjinsha (Tokorozawa)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404275}} (Plum Tenjin) – Established in {{ill|Kōhei|en|Kōhei|WD=Q1193572|lt=Kōhei 6|1=Kōhei}} (1062) by {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|en|Minamoto no Yoshiie|WD=Q32613|lt=Minamoto no Yoshiie|1=Minamoto no Yoshiie}} to pray for victory in the {{ill|Former Nine Years' War|en|Former Nine Years' War|WD=Q1187448|lt=Earlier Nine Years' War|1=Former Nine Years' War}}. * {{ill|Ichihime Shrine|ja|市比売神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichihime Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135578912|1=Ichihime Shrine}} * {{ill|Ashio Shrine|ja|足尾神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashio Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Atago Shrine (Kyoto)|en|Atago Shrine (Kyoto)|WD=Q713021|lt=Atago Shrine|1=Atago Shrine (Kyoto)}} * {{ill|Wakazumi Shrine|ja|別雷神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakazumi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Fuji Shrine|ja|富士神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuji Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Awashima Shrine (Wakayama)|ja|淡島神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awashima Shrine|qq=}} File:Ota-tenmangu in Wakamiya-hachimanhu (Hitachiota).jpg|Kitano Tenjin Shrine File:Keidaisha1 in Wakamiya-hachimangu (Hitachiota).jpg|Ichihime Shrine, Wakazumi Shrine, Atago Shrine File:Ashio-jinja in Wakamiya-hachimangu (Hitachiota).jpg|Ashio Shrine File:Fuji-jinja in Wakamiya-hachimangu (Hitachiota).jpg|Fuji Shrine == Access == The shrine is approximately 1.5 km north of {{ill|Hitachi-Ōta Station|en|Hitachi-Ōta Station|WD=Q1305575|lt=Hitachi-Ōta Station|1=Hitachi-Ōta Station}} on the {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|WD=Q499071|lt=East Japan Railway Company|1=East Japan Railway Company}} (JR). It is a 20-minute walk. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines in Japan|en|List of Shinto shrines in Japan|WD=Q2517815|lt=List of Shinto shrines in Japan|1=List of Shinto shrines in Japan}} * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|en|List of Japanese deities|WD=Q3240847|lt=List of Japanese deities|1=List of Japanese deities}} == External Links == * {{Instagram|wakamiyah}} * {{Twitter|wakamiya_h}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Ibaraki Prefecture Shrine Index}} {{translated page|ja|若宮八幡宮 (常陸太田市)|version=101968922|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[zh:若宮八幡宮]] {{Commons category|Wakamiya-hachimangu (Hitachiota)}} [[ja:若宮八幡宮 (常陸太田市)]] {{wikidata link|Q11616252}} [[Category:Architecture of Hitachiōta City]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Emperor Nintoku]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Municipal History of Hitachiota]] [[Category:Satake clan]] [[Category:Satake Ukyō-no-Daibu Family]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:pages]]

The shrine's Rank under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|en|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|WD=Q712534|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. == History == The shrine was established during the {{ill|Ōei|en|Ōei|WD=Q1193078|lt=Ōei era|1=Ōei}} (circa 1400) by {{ill|Yoshihito Satake|ja|佐竹義人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshihito Satake|1=Satake Yoshihito|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11383421}}, who invited its deity from {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|en|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|WD=Q701403|lt=Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|1=Tsurugaoka Hachimangū}} in {{ill|Kamakura|en|Kamakura|WD=Q200267|lt=Kamakura|1=Kamakura}}, {{ill|Kanagawa Prefecture|en|Kanagawa Prefecture|WD=Q127513|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}. It was originally enshrined within {{ill|Ōta Castle (Hitachi Province)|ja|太田城 (常陸国)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōta Castle (Hitachi Province)|1=Ōda Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5369726}} as a guardian deity and served as the prayer site for the {{ill|Satake clan|en|Satake clan|WD=Q2975363|lt=Satake clan|1=Satake clan}}. Research suggests that the Satake clan initially revered a {{ill|Hachiman shrine|lt=Hachiman shrine|lt=Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八幡宮}} derived from {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|en|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|WD=Q710098|lt=Iwashimizu Hachimangū|1=Iwashimizu Hachimangū}}, which was the guardian shrine of the Minamoto clan in Hitachi. However, Yoshihito Satake, who had ties with the {{ill|Uesugi clan|en|Uesugi clan|WD=Q1150058|lt=Uesugi clan|1=Uesugi clan}}, instead adopted the enshrinement of Tsurugaoka Hachimangū, possibly against opposition from the Satake family.Teiko Tsutsumi, "Satake Clan and Hachiman Worship," {{ill|Ibaraki Prefectural History Museum Bulletin|ja|茨城県立歴史館報|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ibaraki Prefectural History Museum Bulletin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} No. 28, 2001. Compiled in {{ill|Research Series on Medieval Kanto Warriors Vol. 30: Hitachi Satake Clan|ja|シリーズ・中世関東武士の研究 第三十巻 常陸佐竹氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Research Series on Medieval Kanto Warriors Vol. 30: Hitachi Satake Clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Kōshōshō Publishing, 2021), pp. 192–227. Although the Satake clan was later relocated to {{ill|Akita|ja|秋田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita|1=Q11256953|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11256953}} after the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara|1=Battle of Sekigahara}}, they took the shrine's deity with them to {{ill|Ōdate Castle|ja|大館城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōdate Castle|1=Ōdate Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11441913}} as its guardian spirit. The deity was subsequently enshrined at {{ill|Ōdate Hachiman Shrine|ja|大館八幡神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōdate Hachiman Shrine|1=Ōdate Hachiman Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21606446}}, continuing its role as a protector.{{Cite web|title=八幡神社 |url=http://akita-jinjacho.sakura.ne.jp/tatsujin_etc/kennsaku/oodate/67_hachiman_hachiman.html |publisher=Akita Prefecture Shrine Association |accessdate=2015-10-12}} In {{ill|Keichō|en|Keichō|WD=Q1328185|lt=Keichō|1=Keichō}} 14 (1609), the first {{ill|Daimyo|en|Daimyo|WD=Q61982|lt=daimyō|1=Daimyo}} of the {{ill|Mito Domain|en|Mito Domain|WD=Q1146904|lt=Mito Domain|1=Mito Domain}}, {{ill|Tokugawa Yorifusa|en|Tokugawa Yorifusa|WD=Q1133680|lt=Tokugawa Yorifusa|1=Tokugawa Yorifusa}}, was healed of an illness after praying at the shrine. Thereafter, the {{ill|Mito Tokugawa family|en|Mito Tokugawa family|WD=Q842246|lt=Mito Tokugawa clan|1=Mito Tokugawa family}} held the shrine in high reverence. In {{ill|Genroku|en|Genroku|WD=Q1325001|lt=Genroku|1=Genroku}} 5 (1692), the second daimyō, {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|en|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|WD=Q1194944|lt=Tokugawa Mitsukuni|1=Tokugawa Mitsukuni}} (popularly known as Mito Kōmon), designated the shrine as a guardian of the Ōta district. In {{ill|Hōei|en|Hōei|WD=Q1145262|lt=Hōei|1=Hōei}} 5 (1708), the shrine was relocated to its current location alongside the {{ill|Ōta Inari Shrine|ja|太田稲荷神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōta Inari Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which had been worshiped together with it. In 1940 (Showa 15), based on the tradition that a woman named Tsuruko accompanied the deity’s transfer from Tsurugaoka Hachimangū and oversaw its rituals, the shrine's parishioner priestesses revived the "Tsuruko Dance." Recently, this {{ill|Kagura|en|Kagura|WD=Q1348260|lt=kagura|1=Kagura}} offering has been expanded to include performances such as {{ill|Urayasu no Mai|ja|浦安の舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urayasu no Mai|1=Urayasu no mai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11558194}}, {{ill|Toyo no Sakai no Mai|ja|豊栄の舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyo no Sakai no Mai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Yaotome no Mai|ja|八乙女舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yaotome no Mai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 巫女舞|1=miko mai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11479841}}, which are performed with grace during major festivals. [[Ukanomitama no Mikoto]] |established={{ill|Ōei|en|Ōei|WD=Q1193078|lt=Ōei era|1=Ōei}} (circa 1400) |shrine_Rank=Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} |annual_festival=See [[#Festivals]] |main_hall= |map={{location map|Japan Ibaraki|label=Wakamiya Hachimangū|coordinates= {{coord2|36|32|34.8|N|140|31|14.6|E|}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|36|32|34.8|N|140|31|14.6|E}}}} |prefecture={{ill|Hitachiota|WD=Q838663|1=Hitachiōta, Ibaraki}} }} [[File:Torii of Wakamiya-hachimangu (Hitachiota).jpg|thumb|right|245px|Torii]] [[File:Shinmon of Wakamiya-hachimangu (Hitachiota).jpg|thumb|right|245px|Shinmon (gate)]] '''Wakamiya Hachimangū''' (若宮八幡宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Hitachiōta, Ibaraki|en|Hitachiōta, Ibaraki|WD=Q838663|lt=Hitachiōta|1=Hitachiōta, Ibaraki}}, {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|en|Ibaraki Prefecture|WD=Q83273|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|1=Ibaraki Prefecture}}, Japan. The shrine's Rank under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|en|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|WD=Q712534|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. == History == The shrine was established during the {{ill|Ōei|en|Ōei|WD=Q1193078|lt=Ōei era|1=Ōei}} (circa 1400) by {{ill|Yoshihito Satake|ja|佐竹義人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshihito Satake|1=Satake Yoshihito|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11383421}}, who invited its deity from {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|en|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|WD=Q701403|lt=Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|1=Tsurugaoka Hachimangū}} in {{ill|Kamakura|en|Kamakura|WD=Q200267|lt=Kamakura|1=Kamakura}}, {{ill|Kanagawa Prefecture|en|Kanagawa Prefecture|WD=Q127513|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}. It was originally enshrined within {{ill|Ōta Castle (Hitachi Province)|ja|太田城 (常陸国)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōta Castle (Hitachi Province)|1=Ōda Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5369726}} as a guardian deity and served as the prayer site for the {{ill|Satake clan|en|Satake clan|WD=Q2975363|lt=Satake clan|1=Satake clan}}. Research suggests that the Satake clan initially revered a {{ill|Hachiman shrine|lt=Hachiman shrine|lt=Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八幡宮}} derived from {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|en|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|WD=Q710098|lt=Iwashimizu Hachimangū|1=Iwashimizu Hachimangū}}, which was the guardian shrine of the Minamoto clan in Hitachi. However, Yoshihito Satake, who had ties with the {{ill|Uesugi clan|en|Uesugi clan|WD=Q1150058|lt=Uesugi clan|1=Uesugi clan}}, instead adopted the enshrinement of Tsurugaoka Hachimangū, possibly against opposition from the Satake family.Teiko Tsutsumi, "Satake Clan and Hachiman Worship," {{ill|Ibaraki Prefectural History Museum Bulletin|ja|茨城県立歴史館報|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ibaraki Prefectural History Museum Bulletin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} No. 28, 2001. Compiled in {{ill|Research Series on Medieval Kanto Warriors Vol. 30: Hitachi Satake Clan|ja|シリーズ・中世関東武士の研究 第三十巻 常陸佐竹氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Research Series on Medieval Kanto Warriors Vol. 30: Hitachi Satake Clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Kōshōshō Publishing, 2021), pp. 192–227. Although the Satake clan was later relocated to {{ill|Akita|ja|秋田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita|1=Q11256953|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11256953}} after the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara|1=Battle of Sekigahara}}, they took the shrine's deity with them to {{ill|Ōdate Castle|ja|大館城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōdate Castle|1=Ōdate Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11441913}} as its guardian spirit. The deity was subsequently enshrined at {{ill|Ōdate Hachiman Shrine|ja|大館八幡神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōdate Hachiman Shrine|1=Ōdate Hachiman Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21606446}}, continuing its role as a protector.{{Cite web|title=八幡神社 |url=http://akita-jinjacho.sakura.ne.jp/tatsujin_etc/kennsaku/oodate/67_hachiman_hachiman.html |publisher=Akita Prefecture Shrine Association |accessdate=2015-10-12}} In {{ill|Keichō|en|Keichō|WD=Q1328185|lt=Keichō|1=Keichō}} 14 (1609), the first {{ill|Daimyo|en|Daimyo|WD=Q61982|lt=daimyō|1=Daimyo}} of the {{ill|Mito Domain|en|Mito Domain|WD=Q1146904|lt=Mito Domain|1=Mito Domain}}, {{ill|Tokugawa Yorifusa|en|Tokugawa Yorifusa|WD=Q1133680|lt=Tokugawa Yorifusa|1=Tokugawa Yorifusa}}, was healed of an illness after praying at the shrine. Thereafter, the {{ill|Mito Tokugawa family|en|Mito Tokugawa family|WD=Q842246|lt=Mito Tokugawa clan|1=Mito Tokugawa family}} held the shrine in high reverence. In {{ill|Genroku|en|Genroku|WD=Q1325001|lt=Genroku|1=Genroku}} 5 (1692), the second daimyō, {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|en|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|WD=Q1194944|lt=Tokugawa Mitsukuni|1=Tokugawa Mitsukuni}} (popularly known as Mito Kōmon), designated the shrine as a guardian of the Ōta district. In {{ill|Hōei|en|Hōei|WD=Q1145262|lt=Hōei|1=Hōei}} 5 (1708), the shrine was relocated to its current location alongside the {{ill|Ōta Inari Shrine|ja|太田稲荷神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōta Inari Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which had been worshiped together with it. In 1940 (Showa 15), based on the tradition that a woman named Tsuruko accompanied the deity’s transfer from Tsurugaoka Hachimangū and oversaw its rituals, the shrine's parishioner priestesses revived the "Tsuruko Dance." Recently, this {{ill|Kagura|en|Kagura|WD=Q1348260|lt=kagura|1=Kagura}} offering has been expanded to include performances such as {{ill|Urayasu no Mai|ja|浦安の舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urayasu no Mai|1=Urayasu no mai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11558194}}, {{ill|Toyo no Sakai no Mai|ja|豊栄の舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyo no Sakai no Mai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Yaotome no Mai|ja|八乙女舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yaotome no Mai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 巫女舞|1=miko mai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11479841}}, which are performed with grace during major festivals. [[Ukanomitama no Mikoto]] |established={{ill|Ōei|en|Ōei|WD=Q1193078|lt=Ōei era|1=Ōei}} (circa 1400) |shrine_Rank=Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} |annual_festival=See [[#Festivals]] |main_hall= |map={{location map|Japan Ibaraki|label=Wakamiya Hachimangū|coordinates= {{coord2|36|32|34.8|N|140|31|14.6|E|}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|36|32|34.8|N|140|31|14.6|E}}}} |prefecture={{ill|Hitachiota|WD=Q838663|1=Hitachiōta, Ibaraki}} }} [[File:Torii of Wakamiya-hachimangu (Hitachiota).jpg|thumb|right|245px|Torii]] [[File:Shinmon of Wakamiya-hachimangu (Hitachiota).jpg|thumb|right|245px|Shinmon (gate)]] '''Wakamiya Hachimangū''' (若宮八幡宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Hitachiōta, Ibaraki|en|Hitachiōta, Ibaraki|WD=Q838663|lt=Hitachiōta|1=Hitachiōta, Ibaraki}}, {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|en|Ibaraki Prefecture|WD=Q83273|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|1=Ibaraki Prefecture}}, Japan. The shrine's Rank under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|en|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|WD=Q712534|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. == History == The shrine was established during the {{ill|Ōei|en|Ōei|WD=Q1193078|lt=Ōei era|1=Ōei}} (circa 1400) by {{ill|Yoshihito Satake|ja|佐竹義人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshihito Satake|1=Satake Yoshihito|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11383421}}, who invited its deity from {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|en|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|WD=Q701403|lt=Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|1=Tsurugaoka Hachimangū}} in {{ill|Kamakura|en|Kamakura|WD=Q200267|lt=Kamakura|1=Kamakura}}, {{ill|Kanagawa Prefecture|en|Kanagawa Prefecture|WD=Q127513|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}. It was originally enshrined within {{ill|Ōta Castle (Hitachi Province)|ja|太田城 (常陸国)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōta Castle (Hitachi Province)|1=Ōda Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5369726}} as a guardian deity and served as the prayer site for the {{ill|Satake clan|en|Satake clan|WD=Q2975363|lt=Satake clan|1=Satake clan}}. Research suggests that the Satake clan initially revered a {{ill|Hachiman shrine|lt=Hachiman shrine|lt=Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八幡宮}} derived from {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|en|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|WD=Q710098|lt=Iwashimizu Hachimangū|1=Iwashimizu Hachimangū}}, which was the guardian shrine of the Minamoto clan in Hitachi. However, Yoshihito Satake, who had ties with the {{ill|Uesugi clan|en|Uesugi clan|WD=Q1150058|lt=Uesugi clan|1=Uesugi clan}}, instead adopted the enshrinement of Tsurugaoka Hachimangū, possibly against opposition from the Satake family.Teiko Tsutsumi, "Satake Clan and Hachiman Worship," {{ill|Ibaraki Prefectural History Museum Bulletin|ja|茨城県立歴史館報|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ibaraki Prefectural History Museum Bulletin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} No. 28, 2001. Compiled in {{ill|Research Series on Medieval Kanto Warriors Vol. 30: Hitachi Satake Clan|ja|シリーズ・中世関東武士の研究 第三十巻 常陸佐竹氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Research Series on Medieval Kanto Warriors Vol. 30: Hitachi Satake Clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Kōshōshō Publishing, 2021), pp. 192–227. Although the Satake clan was later relocated to {{ill|Akita|ja|秋田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita|1=Q11256953|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11256953}} after the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara|1=Battle of Sekigahara}}, they took the shrine's deity with them to {{ill|Ōdate Castle|ja|大館城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōdate Castle|1=Ōdate Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11441913}} as its guardian spirit. The deity was subsequently enshrined at {{ill|Ōdate Hachiman Shrine|ja|大館八幡神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōdate Hachiman Shrine|1=Ōdate Hachiman Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21606446}}, continuing its role as a protector.{{Cite web|title=八幡神社 |url=http://akita-jinjacho.sakura.ne.jp/tatsujin_etc/kennsaku/oodate/67_hachiman_hachiman.html |publisher=Akita Prefecture Shrine Association |accessdate=2015-10-12}} In {{ill|Keichō|en|Keichō|WD=Q1328185|lt=Keichō|1=Keichō}} 14 (1609), the first {{ill|Daimyo|en|Daimyo|WD=Q61982|lt=daimyō|1=Daimyo}} of the {{ill|Mito Domain|en|Mito Domain|WD=Q1146904|lt=Mito Domain|1=Mito Domain}}, {{ill|Tokugawa Yorifusa|en|Tokugawa Yorifusa|WD=Q1133680|lt=Tokugawa Yorifusa|1=Tokugawa Yorifusa}}, was healed of an illness after praying at the shrine. Thereafter, the {{ill|Mito Tokugawa family|en|Mito Tokugawa family|WD=Q842246|lt=Mito Tokugawa clan|1=Mito Tokugawa family}} held the shrine in high reverence. In {{ill|Genroku|en|Genroku|WD=Q1325001|lt=Genroku|1=Genroku}} 5 (1692), the second daimyō, {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|en|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|WD=Q1194944|lt=Tokugawa Mitsukuni|1=Tokugawa Mitsukuni}} (popularly known as Mito Kōmon), designated the shrine as a guardian of the Ōta district. In {{ill|Hōei|en|Hōei|WD=Q1145262|lt=Hōei|1=Hōei}} 5 (1708), the shrine was relocated to its current location alongside the {{ill|Ōta Inari Shrine|ja|太田稲荷神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōta Inari Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which had been worshiped together with it. In 1940 (Showa 15), based on the tradition that a woman named Tsuruko accompanied the deity’s transfer from Tsurugaoka Hachimangū and oversaw its rituals, the shrine's parishioner priestesses revived the "Tsuruko Dance." Recently, this {{ill|Kagura|en|Kagura|WD=Q1348260|lt=kagura|1=Kagura}} offering has been expanded to include performances such as {{ill|Urayasu no Mai|ja|浦安の舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urayasu no Mai|1=Urayasu no mai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11558194}}, {{ill|Toyo no Sakai no Mai|ja|豊栄の舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyo no Sakai no Mai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Yaotome no Mai|ja|八乙女舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yaotome no Mai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 巫女舞|1=miko mai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11479841}}, which are performed with grace during major festivals. [[File:Kaguraden of Wakamiya-hachimangu (Hitachiota).jpg|thumb|right|245px|Kaguraden]] == Enshrined Deities == * '''[[Ōsasagi no Mikoto]]''' (Emperor Nintoku) * '''[[Ukanomitama no Mikoto]]''' == Festivals == * January: {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|元旦祭|Saitan-sai=Saitan-sai|lt=Saitan-sai (New Year's Festival)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitan-sai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 歳旦祭}} * February: {{ill|Setsubun|ja|節分追儺祭|lt=Setsubun Tsuina-sai|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Setsubun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * May: festival * June: {{ill|Nagoshi no Harae|ja|夏越の祓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagoshi no Harae|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大祓|1=Ōharae-shiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11261910}} * September: Autumn Festival * December: {{ill|Ōharae-shiki|en|Ōharae-shiki|WD=Q11261910|lt=Ōharae|1=Ōharae-shiki}} == Cultural Properties == * Large {{ill|Zelkova serrata|en|Zelkova serrata|WD=Q920344|lt=zelkova|1=Zelkova serrata}} tree (designated a natural monument of Ibaraki Prefecture) * Hanging scroll of Hachiman as a monk * Stone sword artifacts * Military fan (attributed to {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshimitsu|en|Minamoto no Yoshimitsu|WD=Q3083118|lt=Minamoto no Yoshimitsu|1=Minamoto no Yoshimitsu}}) == Auxiliary Shrines == * {{ill|Kitano Tenjin Shrine|ja|北野天神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitano Tenjin Shrine|1=Kitano Tenjinsha (Tokorozawa)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404275}} (Plum Tenjin) – Established in {{ill|Kōhei|en|Kōhei|WD=Q1193572|lt=Kōhei 6|1=Kōhei}} (1062) by {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|en|Minamoto no Yoshiie|WD=Q32613|lt=Minamoto no Yoshiie|1=Minamoto no Yoshiie}} to pray for victory in the {{ill|Former Nine Years' War|en|Former Nine Years' War|WD=Q1187448|lt=Earlier Nine Years' War|1=Former Nine Years' War}}. * {{ill|Ichihime Shrine|ja|市比売神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichihime Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135578912|1=Ichihime Shrine}} * {{ill|Ashio Shrine|ja|足尾神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashio Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Atago Shrine (Kyoto)|en|Atago Shrine (Kyoto)|WD=Q713021|lt=Atago Shrine|1=Atago Shrine (Kyoto)}} * {{ill|Wakazumi Shrine|ja|別雷神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakazumi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Fuji Shrine|ja|富士神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuji Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Awashima Shrine (Wakayama)|ja|淡島神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awashima Shrine|qq=}} File:Ota-tenmangu in Wakamiya-hachimanhu (Hitachiota).jpg|Kitano Tenjin Shrine File:Keidaisha1 in Wakamiya-hachimangu (Hitachiota).jpg|Ichihime Shrine, Wakazumi Shrine, Atago Shrine File:Ashio-jinja in Wakamiya-hachimangu (Hitachiota).jpg|Ashio Shrine File:Fuji-jinja in Wakamiya-hachimangu (Hitachiota).jpg|Fuji Shrine == Access == The shrine is approximately 1.5 km north of {{ill|Hitachi-Ōta Station|en|Hitachi-Ōta Station|WD=Q1305575|lt=Hitachi-Ōta Station|1=Hitachi-Ōta Station}} on the {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|WD=Q499071|lt=East Japan Railway Company|1=East Japan Railway Company}} (JR). It is a 20-minute walk. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines in Japan|en|List of Shinto shrines in Japan|WD=Q2517815|lt=List of Shinto shrines in Japan|1=List of Shinto shrines in Japan}} * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|en|List of Japanese deities|WD=Q3240847|lt=List of Japanese deities|1=List of Japanese deities}} == External Links == * {{Instagram|wakamiyah}} * {{Twitter|wakamiya_h}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Ibaraki Prefecture Shrine Index}} {{translated page|ja|若宮八幡宮 (常陸太田市)|version=101968922|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[zh:若宮八幡宮]] {{Commons category|Wakamiya-hachimangu (Hitachiota)}} [[ja:若宮八幡宮 (常陸太田市)]] {{wikidata link|Q11616252}} [[Category:Architecture of Hitachiōta City]] [[Category:Emperor Nintoku]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Municipal History of Hitachiota]] [[Category:Satake clan]] [[Category:Satake Ukyō-no-Daibu Family]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

The shrine's Rank under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|en|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|WD=Q712534|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. == History == The shrine was established during the {{ill|Ōei|en|Ōei|WD=Q1193078|lt=Ōei era|1=Ōei}} (circa 1400) by {{ill|Yoshihito Satake|ja|佐竹義人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshihito Satake|1=Satake Yoshihito|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11383421}}, who invited its deity from {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|en|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|WD=Q701403|lt=Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|1=Tsurugaoka Hachimangū}} in {{ill|Kamakura|en|Kamakura|WD=Q200267|lt=Kamakura|1=Kamakura}}, {{ill|Kanagawa Prefecture|en|Kanagawa Prefecture|WD=Q127513|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}. It was originally enshrined within {{ill|Ōta Castle (Hitachi Province)|ja|太田城 (常陸国)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōta Castle (Hitachi Province)|1=Ōda Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5369726}} as a guardian deity and served as the prayer site for the {{ill|Satake clan|en|Satake clan|WD=Q2975363|lt=Satake clan|1=Satake clan}}. Research suggests that the Satake clan initially revered a {{ill|Hachiman shrine|lt=Hachiman shrine|lt=Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八幡宮}} derived from {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|en|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|WD=Q710098|lt=Iwashimizu Hachimangū|1=Iwashimizu Hachimangū}}, which was the guardian shrine of the Minamoto clan in Hitachi. Compiled in {{ill|Research Series on Medieval Kanto Warriors Vol. 30: Hitachi Satake Clan|ja|シリーズ・中世関東武士の研究 第三十巻 常陸佐竹氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Research Series on Medieval Kanto Warriors Vol. 30: Hitachi Satake Clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Kōshōshō Publishing, 2021), pp. 192–227. Although the Satake clan was later relocated to {{ill|Akita|ja|秋田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita|1=Q11256953|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11256953}} after the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara|1=Battle of Sekigahara}}, they took the shrine's deity with them to {{ill|Ōdate Castle|ja|大館城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōdate Castle|1=Ōdate Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11441913}} as its guardian spirit. The deity was subsequently enshrined at {{ill|Ōdate Hachiman Shrine|ja|大館八幡神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōdate Hachiman Shrine|1=Ōdate Hachiman Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21606446}}, continuing its role as a protector.{{Cite web|title=八幡神社 |url=http://akita-jinjacho.sakura.ne.jp/tatsujin_etc/kennsaku/oodate/67_hachiman_hachiman.html |publisher=Akita Prefecture Shrine Association |accessdate=2015-10-12}} In {{ill|Keichō|en|Keichō|WD=Q1328185|lt=Keichō|1=Keichō}} 14 (1609), the first {{ill|Daimyo|en|Daimyo|WD=Q61982|lt=daimyō|1=Daimyo}} of the {{ill|Mito Domain|en|Mito Domain|WD=Q1146904|lt=Mito Domain|1=Mito Domain}}, {{ill|Tokugawa Yorifusa|en|Tokugawa Yorifusa|WD=Q1133680|lt=Tokugawa Yorifusa|1=Tokugawa Yorifusa}}, was healed of an illness after praying at the shrine. Thereafter, the {{ill|Mito Tokugawa family|en|Mito Tokugawa family|WD=Q842246|lt=Mito Tokugawa clan|1=Mito Tokugawa family}} held the shrine in high reverence. In {{ill|Genroku|en|Genroku|WD=Q1325001|lt=Genroku|1=Genroku}} 5 (1692), the second daimyō, {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|en|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|WD=Q1194944|lt=Tokugawa Mitsukuni|1=Tokugawa Mitsukuni}} (popularly known as Mito Kōmon), designated the shrine as a guardian of the Ōta district. In {{ill|Hōei|en|Hōei|WD=Q1145262|lt=Hōei|1=Hōei}} 5 (1708), the shrine was relocated to its current location alongside the {{ill|Ōta Inari Shrine|ja|太田稲荷神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōta Inari Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which had been worshiped together with it. In 1940 (Showa 15), based on the tradition that a woman named Tsuruko accompanied the deity’s transfer from Tsurugaoka Hachimangū and oversaw its rituals, the shrine's parishioner priestesses revived the "Tsuruko Dance." Recently, this {{ill|Kagura|en|Kagura|WD=Q1348260|lt=kagura|1=Kagura}} offering has been expanded to include performances such as {{ill|Urayasu no Mai|ja|浦安の舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urayasu no Mai|1=Urayasu no mai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11558194}}, {{ill|Toyo no Sakai no Mai|ja|豊栄の舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyo no Sakai no Mai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Yaotome no Mai|ja|八乙女舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yaotome no Mai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 巫女舞|1=miko mai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11479841}}, which are performed with grace during major festivals.
